axioms.cite: or a plurality (of phonemes, of tokens)

rejected_readings.label: necessary_eternality
rejected_readings.rendering: Any proponent of unity necessarily holds that words are eternal.
rejected_readings.why: Assumes a required link between unity and eternality, violating the orthogonality that the verse establishes through repetition and the commentary confirms: 'unity-theorists and plurality-theorists exist on both sides of the eternality dispute.'

rejected_readings.label: production_requires_plurality
rejected_readings.rendering: A word that is produced cannot be one unified entity.
rejected_readings.why: Falsely links production to plurality, collapsing the two independent questions. Theorists of unity exist on both sides of the eternality/production divide, so production does not entail plurality.

notes: Verse 1.70 establishes a two-dimensional doxographic space. The commentary's repeated emphasis on orthogonality is the key: the eternality question and the unity question must be settled independently, with both positions represented on both sides of the eternality divide. The contract encodes this structural insight while remaining neutral on which position Bhartrhari ultimately favors.
